“But the oil math was always about inbound tankers more than outbound tankers. Without a steady stream of inbound VLCCs, oil production shut-in continues, eating away at the inherent deficit in the global oil balance. It doesn’t matter whether China is reducing imports or refinery runs; the molecule isn’t replaced.

And so far, the inflow is a trickle. No surprise, because the IRGC is firmly aware of the math. During the 60-day negotiation period, they will max out this leverage and throttle the flow under the disguise of granting permits. By throttling permits, they can effectively control who comes in and who goes out. So you would be a fool if you think they don’t know that controlling the inflow of tankers will ultimately decide how much production shut-in returns.

In addition to the logic I explained above, it is now evident, based on discussions with tanker owners, that the tanker rates offered for ships transiting the Strait of Hormuz are astronomical compared with other regions. Not only is the price insanely high, but operators are having trouble assembling crews willing to take the risk of going in.

So no, the issue is far from resolved, yet everyone still thinks it will somehow be resolved because both sides signed an MOU. Just wait until Trump finds out that IRGC is throttling flows and not actually de-mining the Strait of Hormuz. Will he just ignore this reality and play along or will he have to face the inevitable choice of escalation?

I don’t know, and I don’t really care. What I do care is the number of tankers going in, and right now, the market is going to be very wrong about this because the IRGC is going to throttle flows.”

Heavy downside pressure is slamming the base metals complex. On the macro side, it's getting choked out by the USD rally, but the real pain is coming from CTAs aggressively puking shorts across the board.

Yesterday (June 23rd), CTAs stepped on the gas, accelerating systematic selling across the major venues. Kpler data shows CTAs flushed roughly 11,475 lots out of Comex copper and clipped around 2,834 lots from LME copper.

They didn't stop there—they dumped about 1,352 lots of LME aluminum, flipping net short for the first time since September 2025, and they’re continuing to hit the bid in both tin and nickel.

Trump is now turning his attention to refiners and fuel producers, criticizing the slow pace of declines in retail gasoline prices following the sharp drop in crude oil. However, the refining sector's response remains constrained by historically low inventories of refined products, particularly in the middle distillate complex.

As the charts highlight, US gasoline crack spreads remain elevated despite the recent easing in crude prices. The biggest beneficiary of the reopening of Middle East export flows has so far been the distillate market, with gasoil and diesel cracks falling from their March peaks. This reflects the fact that much of the additional crude now reaching the market is medium-to-heavy Gulf crude, which yields a higher proportion of middle distillates such as diesel, gasoil and jet fuel.

While increased crude availability should eventually help ease refining margins and lower fuel prices, depleted product inventories accumulated during the Strait of Hormuz disruption continue to support cracks. The combination of peak summer demand for gasoline keeping prices at the pumps seasonally elevated, refiners – for now - remain incentivized to maximize diesel and jet fuel production, delaying a more pronounced decline in gasoline prices.

text:
Good Morning! 

Futures are up, with ES +27

Oil prices are lower this morning, with WTI crude down to $71 a barrel. 

The techs are looking better this morning. 

South Korea's KOSPI regained ground after yesterday's tech driven sell-off. 

The market is waiting on $MU earnings which will drop later today after the bell. This report will be a critical indicator of semiconductor demand and AI spending. The market can see a big move in the AI tech stocks, led by chips tomorrow. 

$NVDA is leading the Mag 7 this morning. 

$GOOGL will join the Dow Jones Industrial Average, replacing Verizon $VZ, effective Monday, June 29, expanding mega cap tech presence in the blue chip average.

$TWLO is up 4% pre-market on renewed analyst optimism, including a Goldman Sachs Buy initiation with a $300 target, building on the their raised 2026 outlook and AI platform momentum following a successful turnaround.

The AI chip supply chain is facing a critical bottleneck at the manufacturing and testing layer, where optical interconnect scaling and silicon photonics capacity are emerging as key constraints on AI data center buildout. This is bullish for $ALAB $CRDO $AAOI $CIEN $COHR $FN $GLW $LITE. 

I don't see any other notable pre-market activity or significant news. 

I expect to see very little action in the market ahead of $MU earnings later today. 

The market may trade flat and range bound with very little volume for most of the day, with some movement at the end of the day as people do some last minute positioning ahead of those earnings.

US Stocks Set to Recover After Sharp Tech Selloff as Focus Shifts to Micron Earnings

U.S. stock futures are pointing higher Wednesday morning as markets look to stabilize following Tuesday’s sharp tech-led decline. The selloff was driven by weakness in semiconductor and big tech stocks, but investors are now turning their attention to $MU’s upcoming earnings.

Futures Snapshot:
- E-Mini S&P 500 up 0.25% at $7455.75
- E-Mini Nasdaq 100 up 0.58% at $29838.00
- E-Mini Dow down 0.03% at $52064.00

Oil Prices:
- WTI Crude down 1.86% near $71.85
- Brent Crude down 1.69% at $75.50

Key Market Context:
Markets are attempting a recovery after a difficult Tuesday session in which chip stocks and several Magnificent Seven names came under heavy pressure. The rebound in futures is being supported by bargain hunting ahead of $MU’s third-quarter results, which are due after the market close and are seen as a major test for the AI and memory chip trade.

Premarket Movers:
- $MU is up nearly 4% as investors position ahead of earnings. The stock had dropped more than 13% on Tuesday.
- Cerebras Systems is down more than 14% in premarket despite beating expectations in its first earnings report as a public company.
- FedEx is down around 7% after delivering mixed results and cautious full-year guidance.
- Twilio is up 5% after Goldman Sachs initiated coverage with a Buy rating and a $300 price target.
- Wendy’s is surging nearly 14% amid retail investor interest and short-squeeze speculation.

Asia Market Update:
South Korea’s Kospi Index is rebounding strongly after Monday’s roughly 10% plunge. The benchmark rose 3.26% on Wednesday as local investors bought the dip in technology stocks. The Kospi remains one of the best-performing major indices globally this year, up more than 100%.

Economic Calendar:
Today’s key data releases include:
- U.S. new home sales for May (10:00 a.m. ET)
- U.S. leading economic indicators for May (10:00 a.m. ET)
- Weekly crude oil inventories (10:30 a.m. ET)
- $70 billion 5-year Treasury note auction result (1:00 p.m. ET)

Overall Market View:
Sentiment is showing signs of stabilization after Tuesday’s tech rout, with investors shifting focus toward $MU’s earnings as a potential catalyst for the semiconductor and AI sectors. While the broader market remains cautious, the combination of oversold conditions in some tech names and expectations around $MU’s results is supporting a modest recovery in futures. Markets will be watching closely to see whether $MU can deliver strong enough results to restore confidence in the AI trade.

WTI crude oil taps early-March lows near $71/bbl. The shipping recovery in the Strait of Hormuz and slowing global demand are crushing any rebound momentum. 

🟢 Goldman Sachs (The Moderate):
Expects WTI to target ~$75 by Q4, with a full-year average range of $70–$78. They see high-level volatility persisting as geopolitical wildcards linger.

🔴 JPMorgan (The Bear):
Sticking firmly to its bearish outlook, forecasting a major slide down to $55–$58.

What Oil/LNP disruption? Goldman says one factor will send crude prices plunging in the coming year. Goldman Sachs says rising EV adoption will push the price of oil down through 2027.
Tensions around the Strait of Hormuz could still keep prices in flux, but Goldman says they're pointed down. Two factors - the Iran War and EV sales.
EV sales are surging in the aftermath of the Iran war, the analyst say.
The oil shock that boosted crude prices to their highest level in years could soon be a distant memory.

Goldman Sachs says that as the Iran war seems to be winding down and oil shocks fade, the direction of crude between now and the end of 2027 will be lower, spurred by a big consumer shift.

Daan Struyven, co-head of global commodities research at Goldman Sachs and a team of analysts, wrote that they see the war-driven oil shocks leading to a surge in demand for electric vehicles, ultimately putting downward pressure on oil prices.

"We estimate a potential hit to global oil demand in December 2027 of 0.13-0.32mb/d (or 0.1-0.3% of global demand), depending on the persistence of the realized EV car sales acceleration," Struyven wrote.

EV sales had declined significantly recently, sparking speculation that the elimination of federal tax incentives would continue to crush the market. But as Goldman highlighted in the note, EVs have come roaring back globally in response to the surge in gas prices caused by the Iran war.

What smart people are saying about oil prices
©Brandon Bell/Getty Images
The Iran war sent oil up — then down — in volatile trade, rattling markets and stoking inflation fears.
Gulf producers have cut output amid shipping constraints, raising recession concerns.
Analysts warn the rally could echo a 1970s-style oil shock.
Oil futures have swung sharply in rollercoaster trading this week, as investors react to the risk of a prolonged disruption to crude shipments through the Strait of Hormuz.

Brent and West Texas Intermediate crude futures breached the key $100-a-barrel level after trade opened on Sunday evening and have since fallen sharply after President Donald Trump suggested the Iran war could be over soon.

Traffic through the critical oil chokepoint remains constrained a week after the US and Israel attacked Iran, while several major Gulf producers have begun slowing output.

Oil prices have nearly doubled in just over three months in 2026, raising fresh concerns about inflation and the outlook for the economy and stock market.

Here's what energy, business, and finance experts are saying about oil's break above the key $100 a barrel psychological barrier.

🐦 Z Pulse

• Oil Collapse: Brent crude falls to $75, lowest since February. Trump orders DOJ probe into oil companies for price manipulation while prices crater. Conflicting narrative on who's inflating what.

• Turkey MSCI Ultimatum: MSCI demands transparency reforms (ownership disclosure, market surveillance) by November 2026 or Turkey faces potential index removal. Heavy accusations per local analysts.

• $MU Earnings Watch: Micron reports today, market in wait-and-see mode. AI chip sector whipsawed after $NVDA AGM and margin concerns at peers like $CBRS. Space stocks ($ASTS, $RKLB) struggling while AI infrastructure attracts capital.

• AI Chip M&A: $QCOM acquires Modular Inc. for ~$4B to enable cross-platform AI software deployment. SK Hynix Nasdaq listing July 10, targeting $30B+ raise for memory chip capacity.

📰 News Flow

• Iran-Dollar Talks: Treasury Secretary Bessent says US-Iran negotiations include Tehran invoicing oil in dollars. Goldman cuts Q4 crude forecast to $80 citing US-Iran interim peace deal. IAEA dismisses "war of words," expects resumed inspections.

• Data Center Power Rush: $META and $MSFT lead $850B boom in data center leases. Multiple partnerships announced: $TSLA/$RUN team on AI data center energy, FuelCell/$FCEL partners with Fit Energy, Canadian nuclear firm AtkinsRealis seeks US approval for AI power tech.

• China Mineral Controls: Beijing detains two Japanese nationals over alleged rare earth smuggling, launches whistleblower hotline for export violations. Escalating minerals trade enforcement amid tech rivalry.

📊 Market Direction Read

• Bias: Cautious — Futures mixed, oil plunging, tech recovering from 3%+ $NDX selloff but awaiting $MU earnings as "gut check" for AI trade.

• Triggers:

• $MU earnings (afternoon) — memory pricing/AI demand signal for entire chip sector
• Oil volatility — Brent at $75, Trump DOJ probe vs. Iran deal conflicting narratives
• DXY above 101 pressuring gold, crypto ($BTC testing 59-60k support)

• Risk: $MU disappointment could reignite AI infrastructure skepticism after margin pressure at $CBRS; oil collapse may signal demand weakness beyond geopolitics.

🎯 On Watch Today

$MU earnings after close. Dan Ives (Wedbush) calls it a "gut check moment" for markets. Memory chip pricing and AI capex outlook will set tone for semiconductor complex heading into month-end.

Goldman Sachs cuts Q4 crude forecast to $80.

→ Gulf oil exports rebound to 60% of pre-war levels.
→ Markets question need for large security premium.
→ EV surge raises doubts on oil demand recovery.

Watch energy stocks, inflation trends, and currency moves.

Does geopolitical risk come out of barrels next?

Hey @grok is there record oil flowing through the Strait of Hormuz?

No, there are no records of oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz right now—flows remain well below pre-conflict levels (around 20 million barrels per day of crude and products), though they have rebounded somewhat in June 2026 amid a fragile ceasefire.05
Pre-Conflict Baseline
•In 2025 and early 2026 (pre-late February conflict), the strait typically carried ~20–21 million barrels per day (bpd) of crude oil, condensate, and petroleum products, plus significant LNG. This represented about 20–25% of global seaborne oil trade.18
•EIA data for Q4 2025 showed ~20.7 million bpd; flows dropped sharply after the US-Israeli strikes on Iran began around Feb. 28, 2026.35
Recent Situation (as of mid-June 2026)
•Visible/AIS-tracked flows have been much lower for months due to blockades, tensions, “dark” (AIS-off) transits, and workarounds like pipelines (e.g., Saudi East-West to Yanbu, UAE to Fujairah). Estimates for May/early June: often 1–3 million bpd tracked, with clandestine/“ghost” flows pushing totals higher but still far below normal.2
•Non-Iranian flows rose ~50% in early June to at least 1.8 million bpd (from ~1.2 million in May), per Vortexa data—figures often revised upward with satellite analysis.8
•US officials (e.g., Energy Secretary Chris Wright) claimed ~7 million bpd in mid-June (roughly half the pre-war gap, and rising), aided by naval escorts and a reported “secret project.” Independent trackers (Kpler, Vortexa, JPMorgan) generally show lower verifiable numbers and dispute the highest claims.5
•Post-ceasefire (around mid-June), there have been spikes: e.g., claims of 12+ million barrels in one night/day (a high since the conflict began, with Saudi supertankers and others moving), plus increased traffic as confidence returns. Stranded oil is flowing out, but full recovery is expected to take weeks/months.13
•Iranian exports were heavily restricted earlier but have shown signs of resuming.
Context on “Records”
•No sources report flows exceeding or even matching the pre-war ~20 million bpd average. Recent activity reflects partial recovery and catch-up of stranded cargoes after disruption (described as the largest oil supply shock in history, with cumulative Gulf losses in the hundreds of millions to over 1 billion barrels).3
•Data is uncertain due to dark fleets, spoofing, and conflicting claims from US/Iran sides. Independent firms like Kpler, Vortexa, and EIA emphasize revisions and undercounting.7
In short, oil is flowing again and increasing (especially non-Iranian and post-deal), but it is not at record levels—it’s a partial rebound from severe wartime disruption. Markets have adapted somewhat via bypasses and inventories, keeping prices from spiking as much as initially feared. For the absolute latest, check sources like EIA, Kpler, or Vortexa, as the situation evolves quickly.

Your post has the classic Crypto Twitter style — dramatic claims (“everything crashing”, “$5 trillion wiped out”, “end of the business cycle”). For serious investors this is mostly emotional noise. Here are the facts as of June 24, 2026.
1. Hawkish Fed + DXY After the June 17–18 FOMC, more officials expect a 2026 rate hike. DXY rose to multi-month highs, pressuring risk assets. The economy remains resilient though — this is normal repricing after a long risk-on period.
2. Oil crash & 2007 parallel Oil dropped sharply (Brent ~$78) after US-Iran deal hopes and Strait of Hormuz reopening. This removes geopolitical premium and is generally bullish (cheaper energy). Unlike 2007, today’s drop comes from de-escalation, not demand collapse.
3. Euphoria & $2T company SpaceX lost ~$600B market cap post-IPO and now trades near $2T. AI/tech valuations were stretched. On June 23 Nasdaq fell ~2.2%, S&P ~1.4%, Micron -13%. This is a concentrated correction in high-beta names after a big run-up — not a broad crash. Previous weeks saw gains.
Overall picture Sell-off focused on AI/tech. BTC ~$62.5k (down ~2%). Gold/silver down logically with stronger dollar. The $5T wipeout claim is overstated.
For serious investors Weekly moves are normal noise. Focus on quarters/years, fundamentals and risk management. This is a healthy correction in overextended valuations after a strong rally + hawkish shift. Oil’s drop is positive for the economy. Dips can be opportunities if you have long-term conviction. Risk management matters more than panic.
Markets move in cycles. Disciplined investors profit from them — they don’t chase dramatic tweets. DYOR. Not financial advice.

Crypto

Rates / Macro

The main focus today is whether tech can stabilize while the dollar stays firm and commodities continue weakening. Lower oil helps inflation pressure, but higher-rate expectations are still weighing on growth and precious metals.

Today’s Key Scheduled Data

10:00 AM ET: New Home Sales
10:30 AM ET: EIA Crude Oil Inventories

Also watch major memory earnings after the close, since that can impact AI/semiconductor sentiment.

Market Leadership

Strongest Areas: Select technology, semiconductors, memory, and defensive cash-flow groups.

Weak Areas: Oil, energy, gold, silver, crypto, and high-multiple growth.

Key Themes

The tape is trying to stabilize, but it is not a clean risk-on setup yet. Oil and precious metals are weak, BTC is still below key resistance, and tech needs follow-through to repair short-term momentum.

Important Trading Notes

One-Line Read

Markets are stabilizing, but the tape remains selective as tech tries to recover while oil, gold, silver, and BTC stay under pressure; wait for confirmation before increasing risk.

Key Market Summary – June 24

Nasdaq futures +0.40% as investors bought the dip in tech after yesterday’s sharp AI/chip selloff.
Chip stocks rebounding premarket: ARM +4%, Micron +3%, Intel +2%, though traders remain cautious ahead of Micron earnings tonight, which are seen as a key test for AI demand.
Oil down over 1% as shipping through the Strait of Hormuz continues to normalize, easing supply concerns.
10-year Treasury yield fell to 4.49%, helping support growth and technology stocks.
Yesterday's biggest losers: Micron (-13%), Sandisk (-13%), Tesla (-5%), and Primoris (-21%).
IBM gained 5%+ after a JPMorgan upgrade.
U.S. economic data remained solid: Manufacturing PMI (55.7) and Services PMI (51.3) both beat expectations, signaling continued economic expansion.
Today's key events: Micron earnings after the close, New Home Sales report, and EIA crude oil inventories.
Fed outlook: Markets currently price about a 64% chance of no rate change and 36% chance of a 25bp hike at the July meeting.
Europe mixed: Tech stocks stabilized, but defense stocks were pressured by a sharp decline in Rheinmetall.
Asia mixed: China gained slightly on AI-related buying, while Japan fell as investors worried about valuations and future rate hikes.
Notable premarket movers: Micron, ARM, Intel, and Sandisk higher; FedEx down more than 6% after weak guidance; Cerebras down over 10%.

What Matters Most Today

➡️ Micron earnings and guidance will likely determine near-term direction for tech, semiconductors, and the broader AI trade. Strong results could reignite the rally, while weak guidance may extend the recent pullback.

Because regardless of the price of oil, the speculation is still suggesting prices are going to go up. Gas prices have been too high LONG before the Iran war because speculators are jacking up their price predictions because of Trump's tariffs. 

They feel the tariffs have a destabilizing effect on the economy and will drive up inflation so the "speculation" is that prices will rise.

It's not all that dissimilar from how the fed chair determines the interest rates. They try to figure out where inflation WILL BE and where the economy WILL BE to say if the rates go up and down.

I'm not saying that they are right...just that it is the way of things. Speculators control the price of gas...not the price per barrel. it has some effect, sure, but the speculators are like OPEC...they have more power than people realize.
